Two Falling Dots

Building a gravity simulator from two falling dots to three hundred thousand bodies — one optimization at a time. Everything above is running live, right now, on your GPU.

Scroll down to read ↓

The galaxy at the top of this page is not a video. It is twenty thousand bodies, each pulling on all the others, simulated and drawn by your graphics card about a hundred times per second. The same machinery scales to three hundred thousand bodies in the final demo on this page.

Naïvely, three hundred thousand mutually attracting bodies means three hundred thousand squared force calculations per step — ninety billion. We will do it in a browser tab, on whatever machine you are holding.

There is no single trick. Each section fixes a specific limitation in the previous approach, with a live demo you can poke, drag, and break. By the end, every part of the galaxy up top will have a clear job.

Step 1 · One forceGravity, by hand

Every optimization in this article exists to evaluate one formula faster. Here it is — the only physics we will ever need: 

// acceleration of body i, caused by body j
d   = position[j] - position[i]
a_i += G * mass[j] * d / (|d|² + ε²)^(3/2)

That is Newton's law of gravitation with one engineering patch: the ε² term, called softening. Real gravity goes to infinity as two points touch. Simulated gravity, evaluated in discrete time steps, does something worse: a body skims past a neighbour, the force spikes for one step, and the body gets flung across the universe at an unphysical speed. Softening puts a floor under the denominator — below distance ε, the force stops growing. It effectively blurs each point mass into a small fuzzy ball of radius ε, which is why ε enters the formula squared, as a length.

Aside · Where softening comes from

Astronomers have used this exact formula for over a century. It comes from a density profile Henry Plummer fit to globular star clusters in 1911 — decades before anyone could simulate one. A "softened point mass" is mathematically a tiny Plummer sphere.

Try it. Drag anywhere on the black to launch a body around the star. Then drag the softening slider to its minimum and launch a body straight at the centre — it gets slingshotted unrealistically hard. That spike is the bug ε exists to fix.

One integration detail matters here: update velocity from acceleration first, then update position from the new velocity. This ordering — called semi-implicit Euler — costs nothing extra and keeps orbits stable for thousands of revolutions, where the "obvious" order makes them slowly spiral outward.

Why the update order matters: symplectic integrators

Updating velocity first makes the integrator symplectic: it exactly preserves a geometric invariant of Hamiltonian mechanics (phase-space area) even while getting individual positions slightly wrong. The practical consequence is that its errors push orbits sideways rather than inward or outward, so energy errors oscillate instead of accumulating — orbits stay orbits.

Its close cousin, leapfrog integration, has carried this property through nearly every serious gravity and molecular-dynamics code since the 1960s. A one-line ordering decision is the difference between a solar system that lasts and one that evaporates.

One star and a handful of planets is cheap: each body only feels the star. The trouble starts when everything feels everything.

Step 2 · Everyone pulls on everyoneThe quadratic wall

A real star cluster has no fixed central star. Every body pulls on every other body — the disk holds itself together with its own gravity. And for three or more bodies, no general closed-form solution exists. If you want to know what a thousand gravitating bodies will do, there is exactly one method: step the system forward and watch.

Aside · Chaos, and a simulation made of light bulbs

Henri Poincaré proved in the 1890s that three-body dynamics can be chaotic — sensitive to initial conditions in a way no formula can summarize. That proof, written for a prize offered by the King of Sweden, is often counted as the birth of chaos theory.

The first n-body simulation predates the computer. In 1941, Erik Holmberg arranged 74 light bulbs on a table — two "galaxies" of 37 bulbs — exploiting the fact that light intensity falls off as 1/r², exactly like gravity. He measured the light falling on each bulb with a photocell, used it as the gravitational force, moved each bulb by hand, and repeated. His analog computer correctly predicted that close-passing galaxies fling out tidal tails, decades before electronic machines confirmed it.

With an actual computer, the direct implementation is two nested loops: 

for (let i = 0; i < count; i++) {
  let ax = 0, ay = 0;
  for (let j = 0; j < count; j++) {
    const dx = x[j] - x[i], dy = y[j] - y[i];
    const r2 = dx*dx + dy*dy + eps2;
    const inv = G * mass[j] / (r2 * Math.sqrt(r2));
    ax += dx * inv;  ay += dy * inv;
  }
  vx[i] += ax * dt;  vy[i] += ay * dt;
}

This is correct, and it is the ground truth every later optimization will be checked against. It also scales badly. Drag the slider right and watch the milliseconds-per-step number: the work grows with the square of the count. Doubling the bodies quadruples the work — 4,000 bodies is 16 million force calculations, 8,000 is 64 million, every step, sixty steps a second.

Somewhere on that slider, your machine hit a wall. Note what kind of wall: the results are still correct, and the inner loop is about as tight as this code gets. It doesn't matter. An O(n²) algorithm outgrows any constant-factor improvement. Getting further takes either much more arithmetic or a fundamentally better idea. We'll need both — and the arithmetic is already sitting in your machine.

Step 3 · Parallel workMove it to the GPU

Look at the nested loop again. Body 0's force sum doesn't depend on body 1's — every iteration of the outer loop is independent. That is exactly the shape of problem a GPU is built for: thousands of identical computations that never need to talk to each other.

A GPU is thousands of small processors all executing the same program on different data. So: one thread per body, each thread runs the inner loop. The outer loop stops existing as a loop — it becomes hardware.

One refinement teaches the most important fact about GPUs: they are usually limited by memory, not math. If 100,000 threads each independently read all 100,000 positions from main GPU memory, the memory system chokes. Instead, threads cooperate in groups of 256: the group loads a tile of 256 positions into fast on-chip shared memory, everyone sums forces against the tile, then the group loads the next one. 

var<workgroup> tile: array<vec3f, 256>;  // fast on-chip memory

for (var t = 0u; t < numTiles; t++) {
  tile[local_id] = load(bodies[t * 256u + local_id]); // each thread fetches one
  workgroupBarrier();                                  // wait for the whole tile
  for (var k = 0u; k < 256u; k++) {
    acc += force(tile[k], myPosition);                 // everyone uses all 256
  }
  workgroupBarrier();
}

Each position is now read from slow memory once per group instead of once per thread — a 256× cut in memory traffic. This is the tiled n-body kernel from GPU Gems 3, the "hello world" of GPU computing.

Aside · When gravity needed custom silicon

In the 1990s, astronomers at the University of Tokyo were so starved for this computation that they built chips for it: the GRAPE machines ("GRAvity PipE"), with Newton's force law hardwired into the circuits. GRAPE-4 was, for a time, the fastest computer on Earth at any task — as long as that task was gravity. The shader above does the same job on hardware you already own, because the games industry spent thirty years accidentally building gravity machines for everyone.

Same slider as before. The wall that was at ~4,000 bodies is now tens of times further out. But the underlying problem hasn't changed — watch the pair counter: at 120,000 bodies you're computing over 14 billion pair forces per step, and that number still grows with the square of the count. The GPU pushed the wall out; it didn't remove it. Tiling and parallelism buy constant factors. To get past n² for good, we have to compute fewer forces, not compute them faster.

Step 4 · ApproximationDistant things blur

Stand outside on a clear night. Roughly a hundred billion stars in the Andromeda galaxy are pulling on you — and your body feels them as one pull, toward one smudge in the sky. From far enough away, a cluster of bodies is indistinguishable from a single point holding the cluster's total mass at its centre of mass.

Barnes and Hut turned that observation into an algorithm in 1986. Build a quadtree: recursively divide space into quadrants until each cell holds about one body. For every cell, precompute total mass and centre of mass. Then, to sum forces on a body, walk the tree from the root and apply one test at every cell: 

// the multipole acceptance criterion ("does this cluster blur?")
if (cellWidth² < θ² × distance²) {
  acc += forceFrom(cell.totalMass, cell.centerOfMass);  // one term, whole subtree
} else {
  descend into the four children;                       // too close — look closer
}

θ (theta) is the knob that trades accuracy for speed: how small does a cell have to look — width over distance — before it counts as one point? Nearby regions get opened all the way down to individual bodies; the far side of the disk collapses into a handful of lumps. The sum that took n terms now takes about log n.

Here is the whole walk, one piece at a time. Scroll through it slowly — and scroll back up whenever you want to re-watch a stage:

And here it is live. Your cursor is the test body: every yellow box is a cluster the walk accepted as a single point mass for your position. Move toward the disk's core and the boxes shrink near you while the far field stays coarse. Then drag θ down to 0.1 (strict — many small boxes, accurate) and up to 2.0 (cavalier — five boxes for a galaxy).

Watch the counter while you move: a few dozen cluster terms instead of 3,000 body terms, for a force within a fraction of a percent of exact. Barnes–Hut trades a controlled amount of accuracy for a large reduction in work.

Aside · A three-page paper and its family tree

The Barnes–Hut paper in Nature combined two existing ideas: the quadtree, invented by Finkel and Bentley in 1974 for database queries, and the oldest trick in celestial mechanics — treating a distant cluster as a point at its centre of mass.

A year later, Greengard and Rokhlin pushed it further with the Fast Multipole Method, which reaches O(n) with guaranteed error bounds, and was later named one of the top ten algorithms of the twentieth century — alongside quicksort and the FFT. The shared pattern: impose a hierarchy, do exact work locally and summarized work at a distance. Multigrid solvers, wavelets, and JPEG all play the same game.

It's tempting to declare victory: port the tree walk to the GPU and scale up another order of magnitude. But one real obstacle remains, and it's the tree itself.

Step 5 · The wrong residenceA tree the GPU can't build

The textbook Barnes–Hut tree is built by inserting bodies one at a time: find the body's leaf; if it's occupied, split it and push the old occupant down. Every insertion depends on the tree state left by the previous one. It is a deeply sequential algorithm — and since the bodies move every step, the whole tree must be rebuilt every step, forever.

On a CPU that's fine at small n. But our simulation state lives on the GPU now, so each frame would have to copy every position back to the CPU, build the tree in single-threaded JavaScript, and upload the nodes again. Past ~100k bodies, the round-trip over the bus costs more than the physics. The algorithm isn't the bottleneck — the data structure is living on the wrong side of it.

You could port the sequential build to the GPU with atomics and locks (people have; it is complicated and lock-heavy). Or change the structure:

What if the tree had no pointers to build at all?

Force the quadtree to be complete — every cell split to the same depth, occupied or not — and the entire structure becomes arithmetic. The children of cell (level, ix, iy) are, by construction, cells (level+1, 2ix+dx, 2iy+dy). Nothing to allocate, nothing to link; each level is a flat slab in one buffer. If you've used texture mipmaps, you already know this object: it's a mipmap of mass.

Aside · Mipmaps, 1983

Lance Williams introduced mipmaps in 1983 to stop distant textures from shimmering (the name is Latin, multum in parvo — "much in little"), and they proved so useful that GPUs grew dedicated hardware for them. Reusing a texture-filtering structure as a gravity solver is not a coincidence: both problems summarize fine detail you are too far away to see.

Slide through the pyramid below. Level 7 is a fine 128×128 grid over the live disk; each level above merges 2×2 cells into one, up to a single cell holding the whole galaxy. Every cell stores exactly what a Barnes–Hut node stores — total mass and centre of mass (the pink dots) — and the walk from the last demo runs on this structure unchanged.

The build, unlike the pointer tree, parallelizes completely. Two passes:

Scatter. Every body, in parallel, adds its mass into its cell at the finest level. Thousands of threads hit the same cell at once, so the adds must be atomic: 

// one thread per body — wait-free, order-independent
atomicAdd(&grid[cell],     u32(round(m * FP_SCALE)));          // Σ mass
atomicAdd(&grid[cell + 1], bitcast<u32>(i32(round(m * dx * FP_SCALE)))); // Σ m·dx
atomicAdd(&grid[cell + 2], bitcast<u32>(i32(round(m * dy * FP_SCALE)))); // Σ m·dy

Reduce. Build each coarser level from the one below: every parent sums its four children. One thread per parent, no contention, log₂ passes. A 1024×1024 pyramid is built from scratch in ten dispatches taking about a millisecond. The "deeply sequential" tree build became a scatter and a mipmap chain.

Deep dive: fixed-point atomics — three tricks in three lines

WGSL has no atomic float add, so the scatter uses fixed-point arithmetic: scale to integers on the way in, divide back out on the way out, with the scale chosen so that even "all mass in one cell" can't overflow 32 bits.

Second trick: the offsets are stored relative to each cell's centre. They are bounded by half a cell width, so every fixed-point bit goes to precision instead of range.

Third: negative values ride through unsigned atomics via two's-complement bit patterns — u32 wrap-around addition is signed addition. After the reduce, each cell's centre of mass is recovered as COM = Σm·dx / Σm.

Step 6 · GPU-residentThe whole loop on the GPU

Assemble everything. Each simulation step is a fixed pipeline of ~15 GPU dispatches, and nothing touches the CPU: 

bounds   → where is everything? (parallel min/max reduction)
clear    → zero the grid's atomic accumulators
scatter  → every body adds mass into its finest cell   (the atomics above)
reduce   → build the pyramid, level by level            (log₂ passes)
force    → every body walks the implicit tree           (Barnes-Hut, no pointers)
           …and integrates its own position, in place

Two details matter. The bounds pass re-fits the grid to the bodies every step, so the pyramid always wraps the world even when the galaxy drifts or explodes. And the force pass writes each body back in place — safe, because no kernel ever reads another body's position directly; all cross-body information flows through the pyramid. One buffer, no copy of the universe.

Deep dive: atomically min/maxing floats

The bounds pass needs an atomic min/max over floats, which WGSL also doesn't have. The trick: map each float to an unsigned integer whose ordering matches the float's. For positive floats the IEEE-754 bit pattern already sorts correctly; for negative floats you flip all the bits. After the reduction, the same mapping run backwards recovers the float bounds. Integer atomic min/max then does the rest.

Here is the result. The same θ slider you dragged on a 3,000-body toy now governs hundreds of thousands of bodies. Steps-per-frame multiplies simulation speed independent of your display's refresh rate.

On a recent laptop GPU this sustains three hundred thousand bodies at interactive rates — tens of millions of body-steps per second — where the nested loop from Step 2 would need minutes per frame. Same physics, same softening, same θ you can verify against the toy demos. The speedup comes from several independent changes: 

two loops in JS          ~4,000 bodies        (Step 2)
+ GPU, one thread/body   ~25× more            (Step 3)
+ tiles in shared memory   …included above    (Step 3)
+ Barnes-Hut θ-test      O(n²) → O(n log n)   (Step 4)
+ pointer-free pyramid   tree build: ~1 ms    (Step 5)
+ everything GPU-resident  zero round-trips   (Step 6)
Aside · How the real universe gets simulated

The Millennium Run in 2005 followed ten billion particles of dark matter from shortly after the Big Bang to today, using a tree code descended from Barnes and Hut's, hybridized with a grid method much like our pyramid. Its successors underpin most of what cosmologists believe about galaxy formation. The codes are bigger and the supercomputers run for months, but the moves are the ones on this page: soften the force, build a hierarchy, summarize the far field, keep the data where the compute is.

EpilogueWhat carries over

Strip away the gravity and four useful lessons remain:

1. Walls are algorithmic before they are mechanical. The GPU moved the O(n²) wall; only Barnes–Hut removed it. Measure which kind of wall you're hitting before you buy hardware at it.

2. Approximation is a resource. θ converts imperceptible accuracy into very perceptible speed. Most systems have a θ hiding somewhere — find the precision nobody can see, and spend it.

3. Data structures choose your hardware. The pointer quadtree and the pyramid hold identical information. One is unbuildable in parallel; the other is a scatter and a mipmap. When an algorithm "can't run on the GPU," the layout is usually the problem, not the algorithm.

4. Residence beats speed. The CPU tree build wasn't slow because CPUs are slow; it lost to a round-trip. Keeping a loop on one side of the bus is often worth more than optimizing either side.

Possible next steps: sort bodies along a space-filling curve so neighbouring GPU threads walk the same part of the tree (Morton order — worth another 2–5× from memory coherence alone). Replace the uniform pyramid with a parallel-built adaptive tree (LBVH) when clustering gets extreme. Add a third dimension — quadtree to octree, four children to eight.

The galaxy at the top of the page is still spinning. Scroll back up and you can name everything it's doing: the scatter, the pyramid reduction, the pointer-free tree walk, the in-place integration — a hundred times a second, on whatever GPU you happen to own.

Continued in Part two: Gravity on a Dome — the simulation leaves the plane: quadtrees become octrees, the galaxy gains depth, and the world itself gets bent into a hemispheric dome.

Part two: Gravity on a Dome → · Part three: Sorting Water → · Part four: Worlds from Dust →

Companion article to a WebGPU n-body lab. Standing on the shoulders of: Barnes & Hut (1986) · GPU Gems 3, ch. 31 · PWhiddy/Nbody-Gravity
